speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This first interview with Arthur Sundberg was recorded at his home in Potlatch on July 11, 1975.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it that your parents heard about this mill coming in here and decided to come over here themselves?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, where we lived in Wisconsin, we lived way up in the northeast corner of Wisconsin, just a few miles from Michigan. And of course that was a lumbering area and that's the big economy in that whole part of the country was lumbering years ago. But it began to peter out because when they logged, they logged. They just cut everything and of course the stuff didn't grow back as fast as it is. It isn't like what they're doing nowadays, you know. This selective logging.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were just cutting out then? Just cutting and not replanting?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's right.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then of course the bigger outfits, they began to move west because oh years before, why they'd, as the railroads moved out, you see the railroads were granted every other section on both sides of the track all the way across the country. And of course the railroads had an awful lot of timberland, you know, and of course that was a big inducement for some of these lumbering people too to start moving out this way and they come out and they invested in timberland and they got it for, just imagine, you know, practically for a song because you know values were way low those days.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So they accumulated quite a scope of timber. Well then of course when they started to build this plant, there was several men that worked in the lumber mill in the town where we come from where my dad worked. They moved out here and they worked on construction work and then they went back and they was telling others, you know, about the big money out here, you know. Well, at the time we moved out here in the spring of 99, back in that area where we lived in Wisconsin, men that piled green lumber in the lumber yard say for 10 hours a day got a dollar and sixty-five cents for 10 hours work piled green lumber. And then of course the word come back there that out here they was paying two dollars and a quarter an hour for common labor. Just any common labor, you know, was two dollars and a quarter, a day, I meant not an hour. That is for a 10 hour day. Well, that was quite a thing.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So my dad was a lumber grader back there and he got, as I say, lumber pilers got a dollar and sixty-five cents a day. My dad got two dollars and a quarter a day for grading lumber for 10 hours. Then when he come out here he got three dollars and a half a day. So that was quite a boost, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And that is what brought people out and of course there wasn't any lumber people that were native to this part of the country, you know. They all had to come from back in the Midwest or in the East, you see. And of course the Midwest was, oh Michigan, Wisconsin, Minnesota was the three principal logging areas of the United States at that time. Of course they had lots of towns south but it really wasn't recognized as a logging area at Southern Pine. And so that's how come that people migrated out this way. Well of course they come out here, why there was, they build up, they had five sawmills in Court Lane. And then there was sawmills in Harrison on the lake and at Springston. And then of course up north of Court Lane there was sawmills at Sandpoint and Bonners Ferry and Laclede and sawmills all over the country, you see. And all these people that operating these sawmills and they migrated out here from the Midwest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they all start about the turn of the century?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your family have very deep roots in the Midwest logging? Had your father been there for long?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, my parents they immigrated here from Sweden in the long, about 1890s, you see. And of course my dad had worked at that kind of work over in Sweden because that's quite a logging area over there, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so of course when they come over here, well they just actually moved into the logging and lumbering work, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I just can't help but take you back a little farther here and ask you if you knew why they decided to leave Sweden and come to America.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it was strictly economic because, well, at that time, you know, the boatloads of immigrants they were unloading them at the docks in New York just as fast as they could unload one ship and they'd move another one in and unload that. That's as fast as the immigrants were coming into this country from Europe in particular, you see. Because now of course I wasn't there but then I know this story and of course I was. All of these immigrants, they'd come over and they'd have a big tag fastened to their jackets, you know, because none of them could speak any English, you know. And that'd be their destination would be printed on there, you know, so that these officials could shunt them this way or that way. Well, these labor contractors, they would meet these boats in New York and they would try to induce these people that was coming off in the boat to hire out to them, see. And of course they got paid so much ahead for signing them up, you know. And you can imagine all the miles and miles of ditches that had to be dug in New York and Philadelphia and Boston and all the cities, you know. It was all done by hand labor, you know, for all their water pipes and all their sewer systems. And that was all done by hand labor so it took a tremendous amount of people. And, as I say, they tried to induce these people to sign up with them because every time they did they'd make fifty cents a head or whatever it was that paid at that time, And they made big money on it. But then there was these other people that, you know, now like telling about my folks come. Of course they, my folks were married in this country. They didn't know each other over there. But they had a destination in mind when they left because they knew exactly where they were going, see. And they was heading right straight for Menominee, Michigan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Relatives, friends?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, friends. You know, they corresponded and they had friends there. And, well, my mother, she had two brothers that settled in that part of the country. And, oh, they were around the Chicago area. But they didn't work in the logging and lumbering industry. They were both carpenters and builders. They were just construction people. But then that's the reason they had a destination in mind when they come over, you see. And then, of course, after they get here, well then it's just a matter of, oh, just kind of joining in with the rest of them. And, of course, it's a funny thing about the people that immigrated over in, back in, about the turn of the century. Now we had, the town where we come from was Dunbar, Wisconsin. And it was just a small town. Had one lumber mill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But all the Swedes lived in a little group in one corner of this little town, see. And all the French Canadians, they lived in this other little group. And all the Finns from Finland, they lived in another group. And all the Poles, they lived in this other group. And, you know, it's a funny thing, but you take the, oh, the women in particular, they could live here for, well, I know some of them lived here 50 years or more and never did learn to speak English because they lived in this little community where they spoke their native tongue, see. Of course, the men, they had to go out and work. They had to learn some English, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But that's just the way the people were that immigrated over here, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of relations were there between the, among the different groups? Was there hostility?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it was. Relations wasn't too good. Now, I know from what I had seen myself, we had a few Irish.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And the Irish were kind of cocky, overbearing sort of men, you know. And they always wanted to flex their muscles and, of course, there was an awful lot of drinking alcoholic beverages in them days. And they'd always want to pick a fight. But in that little community where we lived, they'd generally come out on the short end because if they tangled with a fin, they'd get cut from one end to the other because a fin was never known to ever, I think he even slept with his knives. They carried knives all the time, you know, and they used them too. And I don't think that you, very seldom that you could ever see a fin but what he had scars all over himself, you know, where they'd fight among themselves, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so they'd always come out on the short end if they tangled with them, you know, in a drunken brawl, you know. And we had lots of them. There were sure lots of drunken brawls every weekend, you know. You could see them out in the street there just wheelie going at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would usually start fights like that?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just be getting the liquor?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, that's, you know, it's the same thing today. You take people that go into these taverns and bars, some of them that can't hold it, you know, and they get quarrelsome and then they want to pick a fight and there's generally maybe some big guy that's trying to show how big he is and then some little guy will crack him and really lay it to him, see. You know, that's the way it works out. Some little guy will generally just really work that big fella over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for it to get from that to a brawl is still a move or two.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, yeah. There's lots of funny things that goes on. I can recall one Sunday back there in Wisconsin,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: we had, oh, it was quite swampy around there. And we had some, oh, it was quite a few of them big bull snakes around there, you know, and I don't know, there was one kind there that they had, people there called him a pine snake, but they were just a big bull snake, had big spots all over them, you know. But anyway, there was a Frenchman there and he had raised quite a few hogs and he had some land that was in this swampy area, you know, and he had hogs around there. And so he killed one of them during big bull snakes there one Saturday. And these fins, they got out there Sunday morning on the street and they were fighting among themselves and they were really laying it to them, you know. So this Frenchman, he thought he'd break up this brawl, you know, so he'd just come and he had that big bull snake with tail, you know, and he'd come and drag it behind him, you know, and he got down there right in the middle of that bunch and he raised it up and shook it in the air, you know, and looked like he was alive. But, you know, they took after him and he's throwing snakes down and he'd run for home as hard as he could go and he just barely got out and he grabbed a rifle and stood them off because they was going to kill him, sure. And that's one way to start one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any guys back there that were known for being really tough men that had a reputation?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they had a reputation locally, but then it didn't go out very far. Probably people in the next town didn't know about it, you know. But, oh, you know, in that little area there, there was a few that were known to be sort of troublemakers and so on and, of course, most of the people kind of shied away from them, you know, to just keep them getting into trouble. But then that's just the life of the lumbering industry. That's part of the lumbering industry. That's part of it's growing up. And, you know, these loggers, the ones that worked in the woods, they all wore cork boots

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and, you know, they'd never take them off. They'd probably take them off when they went to bed and I doubt that too sometimes. But anyway, if they come in the house or went anywhere, Sunday or any other day, they had them cork boots because that was an emblem of their trade. They were cork boots. Any time that you seen a man with cork boots, you knew he was a logger, you see. And, of course, they were pretty proud of that distinction too because they were supposed to be tough. But then this little town where we lived, they wouldn't allow any saloons in that town, but Pembine was just ten miles from there and it was right on the Sioux Line Railroad and the passenger train used to run. It was just right so that, oh, after work in the evening when they got through work, why, they could get on the train and ride up to Pembine and get off and then drink until midnight and then catch the train back again, you see. Well, by the time they was ready to come back or possibly on, maybe they'd stay overnight and come back on Sunday. Why, on this railroad platform, you know, they'd be pretty well glowed up with booze and, of course, most of them would have a suitcase with them full of booze, but then an awful lot of the times they'd get into a big brawl right on the platform there and they'd really go at her, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I've heard that when a guy was down that he could figure on being spiked with the corks. Oh, that's one reason they wore them all the time. There was nothing fair about fighting. If they could just knock them down, well, then they'd go at them with their boots and, boy, they'd just cut them from one end to the other. They didn't care where's his face or arms or legs or anything. They didn't care where his legs were. They didn't let him be running in every direction. They were tough. You know, I've heard, though, along this line that after a fight like that, that the guys who were fighting wouldn't really hold it against each other once they sobered up. It was something that was forgotten. Chances are when they sobered up the next day, they couldn't even remember they'd been in a fight. They was all cut up.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They wondered, where'd I get this?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, you know, as far as the people migrating out this way from the lumber industry back to you there, there was a, well, the big majority of the people that come out here to work in the lumber industry were Scandinavian descent. There was a few Poles that come out here, but they didn't stay long. Most of them just out here a short while and then they went back again. And I don't know of any of the Canadian Frenchmen ever come out here. They all stayed back there and then, oh, whenever they would leave our area back there, they would probably go back up into Quebec or up in that country, see. But those that come out here were mostly the Scandinavian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I've got one more question. You were just saying about the saloon being ten miles away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Even though it was ten miles away, they were still brawling in the town itself?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, in this town where the saloons were, you see. They'd be on the depot platform waiting to catch the train to go home. And while they were waiting, why then they'd have, of course, when they were sober, they got along pretty good. But they get along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there wasn't actually too much going on in the little town where you grew up itself?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course, there was lots of booze there, but they didn't have any saloons, see. Now then, they shipped in beer on the railroad, you know. And, in fact, the matter there was just about a half mile from our house, there was a spot in the woods there that they called the beer garden. And, you know, they had some beautiful trees back in that country. You know, there was oak and maple and ash and hickory and everything you can think of we had back there. And, you know, trees with leaves on them. And there was an area there where these men had, oh, they laid some logs around where they could sit, you know, and they'd cut an open space in between. And it was probably, oh, 40, 50 yards in diameter in this circle. And it was all trees around there. And that's what everybody in the area, they're designated as a beer garden. Well, every Monday morning us kids would go up there, you know, and we'd gather up the beer bottles and we'd take them down to the depot, you know. And, oh, I think we got ten cents a case for the bottles, you know. And, of course, that's a big thing for us kids because, you know, the only way we'd get any money. But, and then beer kegs, you know, we'd go up there and we'd roll them empty beer kegs down, we got a dime for each one of them. And there was lots of them up there every Monday morning because, you know, they'd ship it in and they'd start in as soon as they got through work Saturday night. And even men with families, they wouldn't even think about going home. They just stayed right there and soaked it up until Monday morning and then they'd shake their head and go back to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder how the wives accepted that kind of thing in those days.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I'd just say that the majority of them didn't know any different. That was just part of life, that was all. It was up to the women to raise the kids and see that they got something to eat and the man, he could go and spend his money on booze. It was a hard life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a company town? Is that why there were no saloons?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, well, of course the company owned the, had the saw mill and they owned the store and, but then everybody owned their own home. But I don't know, for some reason or other, they just never would get any saloons started there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sounds like you didn't really need them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, no, no. And this town was the same way, you know. When they started this town, of course, the company owned everything, all the buildings, houses and everything. But that's one thing that was laid down right from the beginning, there'd be no saloons. Now then, there was down here at Palouse, ten miles away, there was ten or twelve saloons there, up here at Princeton, two, three miles away, there was three saloons up there. So they didn't need it, you know, they had lots of saloons around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a warehouser operation back there?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That wasn't a warehouser?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That was a Wells Lumber Company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, was there a lot of broken families in that kind of a situation there?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, I never heard of a divorce until, gosh, I don't know, I guess I was grown up before I ever heard tell of anybody getting that divorce.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, they, they just accepted that as a way of life, that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, most of the people in that town, were they millers or loggers?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was, it was all logging and lumbering, you see. The loggers was out in the woods and of course they had their families in town. And then of course all these people at the sawmill, of course they all lived there, and so that was the main industry, you see. Except that there was, you know, a few worked for the railroad, there was, there was two railroads that went through there. And of course that took some people to, to maintain these railroads, you know, but principally it was lumbering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds as though, to me, that as a kid growing up there, you must have really got to see a lot of what life was all about.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, that's true, you take, I think possibly you might get the same reaction from, from any man my age or older. That kids matured an awful lot quicker then than they do now. You take, oh I think I told you when you were here the other day, you know, that, oh, they'd hire kids twelve years old, you know, to work around the mill. And they'd chew tobacco and smoke and they'd drink and they'd get a hold of it. They couldn't buy it but then they'd get a hold of it. And you take when the average boy was fifteen or sixteen, he was just as much matured as a fellow he is right now at thirty.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's just the way they lived.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Gosh, I knew lots of boys that, in fourth grade in school that chewed tobacco just like any guy you've ever seen.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That was in 1990.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the difference between back there and that kind of lumber town and what you found out?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh well, this here was just like, just like coming into heaven coming out here. There was one thing that was really impressive to us at that time. We left back there on the twentieth day of April and the snow was still all clear up to the eaves on the small, these small houses, you know. And they were still driving their sleighs right across, right over the top of fences and everything that was the twentieth of April. Of course that was a tough winter that year. But when we come out here, it took us four days to get here. Why, the kids were all running barefoot and stuff was green and nice and warm. Boy, that's quite a transition, you know, to go from, in four days time, from what we had there to what they had here. And then of course the, everything was booming here, you know. This mill had been running then for about, oh, two and a half years I guess. A little better.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And they never said no to anybody that had guts enough to ask for a job. They hired, that is, men, they wouldn't, we never heard tell of them hiring women.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But men or boys, if they asked for a job, if they looked like they could work, they'd put them on. And they'd fire them just as easy as they hired them. There was no unions and there was nothing. The boss, he was the kingpin and what he said went. He'd hire you and he'd fire you and he could fire you for the very least little thing too sometimes. But if he didn't like you, well then that was it, you had it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the town itself more attractive? Was there more to it? Did it seem like a better place to live?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take, it was pretty well laid out when they built this little town. In fact they had a board fence around every lot in town. Every lot in town had, oh there was about three one by sixes high, horizontally, you know, with a space in between. And then a cap board on top. So it was a really attractive fence. And every lot had a front gate and a back gate. And of course that was the way people lived those days all over the country. You could go anywhere in the United States, you could find everybody had a fence around their place. And everything was new and painted.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And the company had set out two trees in front of every house out in the street. They were all small but then, oh I think possibly the average tree must have been eight or ten feet tall when they planted them. And they were maple trees and elm trees and you know, different kinds like that. They didn't plant any of the native trees to the country. It was all trees that had leaves on them. Of course the native trees here are all pine and firs and stuff like that. But they really laid it out to make a nice little town out of it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, oh it stayed that way up until about the time of World War I. And just shortly after that, well then of course they first won and then another one would tear out this or tear out that or something. And then of course they had wood sidewalks all over. Every street was a wood sidewalk on both sides of the street and up and down town and every which way. They made lots of lumber and they used lots of lumber too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have an easy time finding a place to stay and starting in here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, right away. Right away we stayed with friends for, oh I don't know, I think about three or four days. But then there was a vacant house that we got right away and then of course we didn't ship our furniture out from the east so we had to buy new furniture. So it was I think about four days there that we had to stay with friends and then we moved into our own house. They were pretty good about that. And of course they had an awful lot of single people or men that didn't have their families here and they had a hotel, a pretty good size hotel. And this hotel had an annex building that was just as big as the hotel and it was just all rooms. And then they had a boarding house. There was a pretty big boarding house.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then just about, oh at least 75 % of the families in town kept boarders. There was that many single people here you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you could get room and board for,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: well some of them got it for $12 a month and of course most of them was about $18 a month for room and board. Three meals a day in a room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the place like, the house like that your parents and you moved into?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well it was a two bedroom, five room house. And we were a little bit crowded but then we made do. But it was, you know it was practically new and it painted nice on the outside and the inside was all papered nice with wallpaper. Of course it just had one water tap inside and it was a cold water tap. The only people that had any hot and cold water, running water in the house were brass, you know. The management of the company, you know, from the manager down through the superintendents and so on. They had houses that had hot and cold running water with bathrooms. But all the rest of them they just had a sink with just a cold water tap. And of course they had to heat all their water on the stove and everybody burned wood them days, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you telling me about how the drain system worked? Drains?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Drains underwater.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah it was very simple and easy to construct. They bore a hole in the floor under this sink and they had a piece of pipe that just went down, just cleared the floor in under the house. And that was it. And of course the people that lived in the house, why if they didn't want to stink, why they'd crawl under there and they'd build a wooden box. And this would drain into this wooden box. It was generally two 1x6 boards and two 1x4 boards and that would make about a four inch box, you see. And they would run that out through to where it got out from underneath the house. And then it'd just run on an open ditch out to find its own way down downhill, you know. And if it happened to be into the neighbor's yard or into the street, well it'd just put you every way it went. And of course if the neighbor didn't like it, well he could ditch it to make it go out into the street. But that was it. Flies thicker than the dickens, you know. Of course as far as flies were concerned, that is, oh I don't know, I don't think you could find any place anywhere in the United States, Charlie. Flies were just as thick as a cloud, you know. And everybody had screen doors. Most everybody had screen doors. And of course the screen door, if you were on the outside and was going to go in, why, this door would be just literally covered with flies and you'd have to shake the door a few times, shake them off and then tuck in. Otherwise you got them all in the house, you see. And if you was on the inside going out, you'd have to shake them a little while before you'd go out so you wouldn't let them in. Well then they, they invented something real, real wonderful. They invented some stuff they call fly paper.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh gee, everybody was happy when they got that stuff.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They'd come in sheets about, oh about 10 by 14. And they were two sheets together, you see, with the stick them in between. And of course they'd done that so that you could handle them, you see. And you bought a sheet of fly paper, or ten sheets of fly paper, whatever you wanted. And then of course when you get home while you're taking, you'd pull these apart. And this gooey in between, boy you didn't dare touch it because it'd hang right to you. And you'd lay one down here, and you'd lay one up there, and lay one over here. You had them laying all over.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you know, it's just a matter of minutes or even hours until they'd be literally covered with flies. And then of course you'd burn that and lay out another one. And you still had lots of them flying in the air. Well then somebody improved on that, and they made some that would come in a little tube about three inches long, and about an inch or so in diameter. And you'd open that up, and this was all curled up in there, and you'd pull that out, you know. And then you could hang them in the ceiling, you see. And there would be just a curl hanging down, but they had this gooey on there. Well that was alright until somebody was tall enough to hit one of them with his hair, and then he'd get that thing stuck in his hair.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, with Potlatch, did Moscow have the same situation with the drainage, or did they have the sewer?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they did, but then of course as the town started to grow, then they of course had to put in sewage systems, you know. But of course you'd have to go back a little bit further than what I'm speaking about here, because the way it was built toward Moscow was just like any other little town starting, you see. It was just a few houses, a house here and a house there, and scattered around. Each one of them had a privy out in the backyard, and each one of them had their own well, and each one of them just put it there. In fact, the matter is, there was, most of the, oh, for instance the women used to, had a big dishpan, and they'd fill that with water and set it on the stove. And then when it got hot enough, they'd put their dishes in there and wash the dishes.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you take the average house, I'm just speaking about ordinary working people, now I'm not talking about somebody that's got a lot of finesse, but just ordinary people. Well, I've seen where that dishwater, it got pretty thick before they sold it out, because, you know, they had to go out to the pump, you know, and pump a bucket full and pack it in, you know. But then when they did throw it out, they'd just open the door and, out they'd go. Well, that's one reason they had all them flies hanging around the door, see, because just outside there was all this stuff that they were just throwing out, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the outhouse arrangements in Potlatch?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they were very modern compared with the ordinary rural living. You take, rural living used to be that they would dig a hole about two feet wide and maybe three or four feet long and then maybe six feet deep. And then they'd build a little building on top of that, you see, and then they'd generally put in a crescent or a star or something in the door to indicate what it was. But when Potlatch built, they built a double woodshed. One building served two houses, you know, they built it so that about the center of the building was beyond the line of the lot, you see. And it had a wall partitioned in there so that this house had this part of the building and this house had this part of the building for a woodshed, see. And then on the end of the woodshed, which was part of the building, was this little privy building, see. And it was a two-holer, but instead of having a hole in the ground, it had a drawer of a wooden box there. Well, then they had a contractor that'd come around once a month and he would empty all these, you see, and cart it off and get rid of it. So, you see, they were a little bit more modern than the average rural person because they didn't have that hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They used wooden boxes, I would imagine.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, yeah, wooden boxes. And then this contractor, he'd always come around about midnight to make you around, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the insulation in the houses?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard complaints about the lack there. There was no insulation at all.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: In fact, the matter is they... It's just in recent years, you know, when they started using insulation in houses, up until just a very few years ago, it didn't make any difference whether the house was built here or in Moscow or in Spokane or New York or anywhere else. They never knew anything about insulation, you know. And, of course, if it was an all-frame building, why... And it was a four-quarter board, usually six inches in width, some of it was eight. But then it was tongue and grooved and then it was shaped out, you know. You've seen that where they were kind of scooped out. Well, that's what they called drop-siding. Well,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: as time went on and this lumber would expand and contract, you know, from the weather, it developed cracks, you know. That is, it would be gaps where this...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: This tongue here on this bottom board was supposed to go up into the groove on this upper board, you see, to make a seal. Well, some of them would, if the lumber wasn't thoroughly dry, when it was put on, why it would pull apart, to where there would be just a little crack in between here where the wind could go through. And then, of course, the inside of the... most of the houses were just one by twelve boards that weren't matched. And, but then they put on a building felt and then wallpaper. Well, as long as all that stayed tight, it was pretty good. But then if it got loosened up, then sometimes the wind would make this whole wallpaper go back and forth like this, you know, some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you could see it, huh?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But that's just all part of life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would you say the houses were well constructed for those days or hastily constructed?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they were constructed according to the...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you might say the architecture of the times, you know, for that style of a house. You know, they'd say, well, now we're going to build a hundred houses here and we're going to pay $250 a piece for each one of them. You know, that haven't been built. That is what they'll cost. Material and labor and all of the houses cost $250. Maybe they'll make so many it'll cost $300. But about $300 would be about the average cost of a house at the time they were built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, were people that could only rent at the time, they couldn't buy these houses? And the renting was pretty reasonable when you came?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. The rent was... Well, it was, you know, everything was prorated according to what the times were and what they paid for wages and all that. But everything was very reasonable. You know, oh, the average house rented for possibly $8 or $10 a month, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And that was water included. And, of course, there's one thing about... that we were impressed with, too, when we come out here, you know, especially as kids. Where we lived in Wisconsin, everybody used coal oil lamps. And when we come out here, every house had electricity. But there was just one drop light in each room, you know. And, of course, you could screw in whatever size bulb you wanted in there, you know, for lights. And then, of course, they had these carbon arc street lights on all the streets. So the streets were lit up good and it looked pretty good to us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your folks didn't have any qualms about coming to live in a real company town?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, it was... as far as owning your own home or renting, that didn't have any bearing at all. Because, in fact, the matter is, you could... up until the time that the company sold these houses, you could rent so much cheaper than you could own it. It was no comparison at all. Now, you take this house here. We rented this house for quite a few years before they sold. And we paid $20 a month. Well, now, we pay $225 a year taxes and we pay $100 a year insurance. And then, if it needs painting or any upkeep or anything, you pay all that. So you couldn't possibly own a house for what we paid rent for, you know. But there's a lot of satisfaction in knowing that you own a house, you know. That's the only thing. But as far as costs are concerned, you couldn't beat it, you know, when you was paying rent. Because every five years, about or even sooner than that if it was necessary, they would come around and they would redecorate your house on the inside and then they would paint the outside. And if the sidewalks needed repair, of course, it was all wooden sidewalks. Why, they kept the sidewalks in real good repair all the time.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they take care of general, of everyday type maintenance if you needed help with it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, you take, you know, they kept their own plumber and they had their own electricians and all that. If you had faucet leaking in the kitchen sink, you just go down and tell them or call them on the telephone or something. He may not come right that instant, but then it wouldn't be long. He'd be up, he'd fix that drippy faucet or anything else that was needed. And they did, they were real good about maintaining all those things, you know. As far as the Company Town is concerned, you know, just the name Company Town, you know, kind of leaves a bad taste in everybody's mouth. Because the fact of the matter is that Company Town, they earned a pretty bad reputation in lots of parts of the country. You take particularly in the coal fields, you know. But as far as Potlatch Lumber Company at that time, and then afterwards Potlatch Forest...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They were real good about... And then they extended credit now. Credit is, you know, looked down on now, nowadays by people. But don't it, you know, people just starting out and people that, you know, just moving into the town or into the area and getting a job. Boy, without credit, it'd be pretty tough to get along. Sometimes it'd be hard to find enough to eat on, you know, as far as that goes until you can get a payday. Well, it was for quite a few years that payday was just once a month. But then they had a script system that wasn't compulsory and it was just a matter of helping the people out that wanted it. But they could go down to the office and... Just say for instance now that you went to work this morning and tomorrow morning your wife had to go to the store and get groceries. Or anything that was within the town for sale. And of course you didn't have any money because you just started to work. She could go down here at the office and get what they called a coupon book. That is a script, you see. And she could get that in denominations of $2 or $5 or $10 or $20. They had four denominations of these script books, you see. And then they had these little coupons you could tear off, you know. And each one of them was stamped on there at the value, you know. There were some of them that was one cent value. Others were five cents. Others were ten cents and twenty-five cents and fifty cents. And if you got, you know, say a twenty dollar coupon book, there'd be one page in there with each coupon. You'd have a one dollar value, you see. And of course you could, like if you went to work this morning, well tomorrow you could go down and draw one of the coupon books, you know, for up to the amount that you had coming. And she could go to the store and buy whatever she wanted. Just the same as if you had cash. Well, a lot of people looked down on that, you know. That is, people that weren't involved, but the people that lived here, there was a lot of people that used those. And they, it seemed like they just couldn't live without them. And then of course, there was an established credit. Well then of course they would buy and buy it on credit, discharge it. And then they'd pay once a month. They got paid once a month, so they'd pay the store once a month. Or any other, well, you know, oh for instance now the milkman of course would be an independent. And the milkman, he would accept these, the script, you know, these coupons. And then they had an independent store over here in Onaway. And they would accept these coupons, script. And of course they could cash them in, take them down here and they'd turn them in for cash. Or this store down here done a tremendous volume of wholesale business too at that time. So of course maybe this little store over here would get a lot of their supplies from down here, you know. And they could turn that in or they could take these coupons and take them right to the bank and deposit them in the bank just the same as they would any other form of money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was no loss of value in the service charge to use them?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was used strictly as an accommodation to the people. And then of course it was a big help to any independent merchant too because he didn't have to give credit. See he could take these coupons, he could just take these coupons every day and put them in an envelope and take them down and deposit them in the bank just the same as he'd deposit cash money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These coupons were given to people based on how much the husband had earned. So if he'd worked for three days then the family could get three days worth of coupons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They would probably withhold up say if they were living in the town and renting they'd see to it that there was enough withheld to pay that rent for that house because the rent for the house was always taken out of your pay envelope on payday because you didn't have that to bother with at all. All you had to do was live there and you didn't have to bother about paying any rent or anything because they took care of all that for you. That was a help in itself. They didn't have to bother with anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had mentioned that to me before about the store doing the large wholesale business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you describe that to me how it happened if they did such a big trade here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course, their wholesale business was, you might say, intercompany because now the company had oh just ever so many logging camps for instance.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Everything was supplied from this store down here. They were the wholesaler and then of course these camps they would put their orders in here and then they would furnish them with meat or loggers' boots or woolen underwear or wool shirts or overalls or wool pants or timber. Timothy hay or oats or horse collars or harness or double trees or single trees or neck yokes. You name it. They handled it right from here.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They had that many carloads of pears and potatoes they'd buy from the farmers. They would probably buy oh maybe 10 ,000 or maybe 20 ,000 hundred pound sacks. They had their own potato warehouse and they soldered them and graded them. They could sell them to Palouse or Moscow or anywhere else.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They were up until about the time of the Depression in the 30s. Up until about the 30s they had, you might say, all of the farm implement business was right here for all the area clear as far as Rosalia, Oakesdale, Colfax, Uniontown, Genesee, Moscow, Derry. All of that was handled right from here. After that they just let it go and now they've got all this farm implement business scattered all over the country. Of course, them days, speaking of, farm implement was all horse drawn stuff, horse drawn plows, walking plows, gang plows, harrows, binders, mowers, rakes and all that stuff. See, there wasn't any of that big stuff like they've got now. But nevertheless, they did have a tremendous amount of business here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say they were probably the biggest wholesaler in the county here in those early days?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they were the biggest wholesaler this side of Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I really don't know whether places in Moscow like David's which had a grocery department, of course, too, as well as their men's, the clothing and all that, whether or not they did much wholesale business at all.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I think David's was strictly a retail business.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Williamson's too. He was the founder.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The fact of the matter is, I think Williamson, he had most of the business in Moscow and that whole Moscow, Troy, Genesee area.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Because he was a merchant that, well, he was just a people's merchant, you know. He talked the people's language and all the farmers and all the logging industry. And over in that part of the country, they all went to Williamson. Now, my wife's folks, they lived over in that Troy area, around Troy and around Joel. And, of course, I've heard my father-in-law tell us many times, you know, about, you know, they've done their business with Williamson, you know, because he was the kind of a merchant that they liked to go to, you know. In another, you take, it's a funny thing about people, you take even today, of course it isn't as prevalent today as it was a few years back, but it still prevails to some extent right now. You take the average person that's just, you know, ordinary, working person, well, they're going down the street, they're going shopping. Well, you go over here to Moscow and David's just got a big display window there, you know, it's all fixed up nice and all that, but David's has got a reputation that goes way, way back. It's kind of a snooty place, so I pass that up, I don't go in there. And Michael Bus, I don't go in there because somebody told me that their stuff is awfully expensive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I walk past Creighton's and I just pass that up because I've been told too that they, you know, you've got to have lots of money to shop in there, so where do I go?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I go over to J .C. Penney's, see. Well, I know, but that's the way people are, you see, and that's the way it was with Williamson when he had a store over there in Moscow, was that you'd go in there and you could probably buy the same kind of overalls over at David's, but farmers had come to town, they had their old dung-y shoes on and their dirty overalls and stuff. So they went over to Williamson and he met them at the door and shook their hand and tickled to death to do business with them and they were tickled to death to do business with him, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think his prices were better for the same kind of stuff?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Did he have less cheaper lines than... Well, he had more people than another, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Now, right to this day, as far as I'm concerned, I have been in David's a few times, but it's very few times that I go in there and you can probably buy in there as cheap as anywhere else. But I just don't like the place, that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Potlatch store? Were its prices good for the local people?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, some people said that they were higher and they probably were in some respects, but... Oh, I don't know, a lot of people thought they might take advantage of you because you didn't have transportation to go someplace else, it was competition, but... You take outside of the... You know, sometimes they'd compare their prices with the Sears-Wilbuff catalog and they'd say, well, it's higher here than it is in the catalog. Well, of course, it's bound to be higher than it is in the catalog. Catalog houses run differently. But down here, they had a real good meat department and they had a big grocery department. They had an extremely large shoe department. You know, there's very few stores anywhere around the country at that time that had as big a shoe department as they had here. They had a big candy and tobacco department. They had a men's furnishing department. They had a women's furnishing department. They had a hardware department and they had a house furnishing department, furniture. Besides, in another building, they had implements. And then they had a jewelry department. That was privately run. A jeweler had his own private business, but he had it in the store. But they had people hired that were hall experts that had a lot of experience in their particular line of merchandise that they were handling, you see. They had men, say for instance, in the men's furnishing department that were just as good as you'd find anywhere in the city, you know, as far as knowing men's furnishings were concerned. Same with the women's department and all of them. It was pretty well laid out and pretty well organized. Now, I worked here, I think I told you the other time here about working here on the railroad. And I handled all the freight and baggage going in and going out. And of course, them days, everything moved by railroad. There was nothing moved at all by automobile.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And they had a sample room down here in the store where they'd be as high as, oh, possibly 40 or 50. The big sample trunks would be in there at one time. And these sample trunks,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: well, each one of them would be about at least three times as big as the average, a real good sized trunk. And they were all filled with samples that these salesmen carried with them, you see. They'd probably be, maybe one salesman would have as high as 10 or 12 of these big sample trunks. And maybe the next one he'd have two or three and maybe another one had four or five and so on. But there'd be that many salesmen that converged in here at a time. And they'd open up these sample trunks and lay out their samples and they bought all their stuff from them, just the same as you would if you went to New York City to buy your stuff, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The people from the different departments would come in and look at it and select what they wanted.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And chances are that they would lay out their samples here, but merchants in Palouse and maybe some of the merchants from Moscow would come over here and look at their stuff, see. But here's where they had them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, that makes me think that probably there was a great deal of new merchandise flowing in and out of the store. I mean, they probably had current lines of goods.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, they did sell a lot of stuff and of course, you take, oh the people then are just the same as they are right now and of course, maybe not as extensive. But everybody, I don't care who it is, they've always got a desire to better themselves in the way of clothing or jewelry or something. You know, they like to, everybody takes a lot of pride in themselves. So, you take, whenever a man thought he could afford it, he'd go in and he'd buy him a new pair of shoes. That's another thing they had here, was a separate department. Well, I told you they had a big shoe department. But anyway, either a pair of shoes or maybe he'd go in and buy a new suit or a new shirt or tie or a new hat, a pair of gloves. You know, and the same with the women. Whenever they could afford it, they'd like to buy some goods to make them a dress or buy one that was already made or a pair of shoes or some stockings. And you know, there was very little money that was saved as far as saving was concerned because the fact of the matter is, oh you know, if you earn $60 in a month and then of course you had your living expenses, it wasn't an awful lot to save out of that, you know. But that would be about an average income would be, well, about maybe $60 to $90 a month, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I want to ask you before we get off the subject of the store about these big sales that you mentioned to me before. You said they were quite an affair.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, this McDonald that was the manager of the store, he was the typical P .T. Barnum. He was a, oh I think he was of Scotch descent. But he was a real handsome man, big man with a big mustache and he was full of blarney from one end to the other. Boy, I'll tell you, anytime that you'd brush up close to him, he would just run off in his chin, you know. He was really good. Well, you know, about twice a year, sometimes more often than that, but about an average I think, maybe twice a year, he'd advertise a big sale. And he advertised it there from Spokane to Lewiston and all over the country, you know. And of course he'd, maybe one time he'd say, well, he had free haircuts. Or maybe the next time it'd be, well, putting it every time, it'd be free coffee and donuts and maybe free candy for the kids or something, you know. Well, in his farm implement business, he'd done an awful lot of trading, you know. He had horses and cattle and secondhand plows and secondhand mowing machines and he had more darn stuff, you know, gathered up, you know, in the six months period. Well, when the sale day come, well, I'd hear it come to people. And they, actually they come as far as Rosalia with horses and, you know, buggies and wagons. Maybe some of them, they can take two or three days for the trip, you know. And they come from Oakesdale and Garfield and clear down as far as Colfax and Uniontown, you know, that's quite a ways off, you know. Lots of them come clear from Uniontown, Genesee. And the hillside over here on the north side of town, you know, where the Forest Ranger station is up there. Well, from there, clear over, almost over the on-way there. That hillside would be just thick, just full of wagons and teams, you know, tied out there. And of course, the town was just full of them. And the streets down there, you just, you couldn't drive through, you know, for traffic for people and teams and stuff. And old Mac, he'd stand there and his belly would bounce up and down and he'd laugh and he'd talk and he'd trade and he'd swindle some of them people into something terrible. And they'd, oh, they liked it, oh, they just really soaked it up there. There's nobody in the world like Mac.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his style? How did he come at you?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, well, he was just jovial, just any other thing. Just pat you on the back, well, gosh, come on over here and have a cigar. And, oh, yeah, gosh, you know, he was, he was a showman, that fella. And he sold a lot of stuff in the sale day, too, I'll tell you, they hauled a lot of stuff out of here. Both new and used, you know. He'd get rid of, oh, you know, he'd have the veterinary to doctor up some of these old, geared up horses that he traded for. And, gosh, they had lots of spirit on sale day. But some of them didn't even make it home, I don't think. It was a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he be selling these big lots, selling these cargo lots at big discounts, too, on the sale?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, he didn't, I don't think he sold anything in carload lots at the sale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, would he get into some items for the sale that he'd buy in big quantities?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah, yeah, you know, he knew pretty much about what would move, you know. And, oh, he'd probably have a big supply of horse collars and collar pads and new harness. And, you know, if a farmer had a real good year and really was going to spurge, well, he'd come down, he'd buy a new set of harness, you know, for his team. And maybe he'd buy more than one set. And, boy, he'd be pretty proud, you know, when he got that new harness on that team, you know. And maybe he'd buy a new wagon, nice yellow wheels, you know, maybe one that's got, wheels had red stripes in them or something, you know. And, you know, just all kinds of gimmicks. And, you know, a person pretty near has to live during the period in order to appreciate how people felt about some of those things. Because, you know, oh, for instance, now, a kid getting a new pair of overalls, a pair that was starched up good and hadn't, wasn't faded out from washing, boy, that kid was as proud as a peacock, you know, with a new pair of overalls, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, nowadays, you know, I even go out and buy a kid a $5 ,000 automobile, you know, make him happy for a day or two anyway. And, but, back there when the economy wasn't what it is now, I, it didn't take too much to make people happy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a big socializing occasion when people would come in?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, well, of course, people were much more sociable year round than they are nowadays, I think, in a simple way, you know. It was always something that didn't involve a lot of money.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, oh, it wasn't, it was just every little while, whether if you'd taken on the summertime in particular, they'd have these ice cream socials. You've probably heard of an ice cream social. I've heard of them. Well, that was their main attraction was this ice, homemade ice cream, and maybe they bought an ice cream, but then a lot of it was they made it themselves. And they'd have cake and ice cream and the people got together and they'd just visit and have a good time and it was what they called a social. And they'd wear their very best and women had ribbons in their hair and great big combs and it really dolled up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the great big comb, you mean the style that they wore the hair in?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, yeah, you take what the women used to put their hair up, you know. Well, just about all the women had these either celluloid or tortoise shell combs, you know, that they put back here, you know. And, oh, the comb would probably have teeth in it about two or three inches long, but then this part up above here would probably be up as high as six inches wide and all decorated, you know. And they had that there and then they had one on each side here, you know, and then a nice great big ribbon, a big bow that big, you know, in the back here. They'd get their ribbon, you know, about six inches wide and make a nice great big bow and put in their hair. That's, they really dolled up, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were they have ice cream socials here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they would, here or any other town, every town was the same thing. If they had a gathering place, a community hall or a church or any place, you know, where they had room enough for people to gather because usually it was public, you know. It was advertised for the public to come. And, you know, they're starting in to have a few of them again. I see here on the television here every once in a while where they, some of these, oh, some of these granges and so on, you know, that advertise are going to have an ice cream social, you know. That's getting back to what it used to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's a lot more community than just family then too.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, yeah, there was a lot of community gatherings, you know, that people would get together and you take when you get out into the rural area, you know, they used to have a lot of fun. They, oh, you take in the fall and winter time especially, maybe at one farmhouse they'd say, no, we're done, come on over, we're going to have a dance tonight or Saturday night. Well, they'd come over and usually they'd just have some scattered, a little wing ding, you know, and they'd have a lunch and they'd have somebody would cord on the organ and somebody would fiddle and, boy, they'd have a real time, you know, for...

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But that's, that was community living.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that Potlatch itself, the town here, had that same kind of a feeling to it? And that same kind of, you know, house sweats?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, they didn't have them here in town so much because, but they did have a lot of public dances because they had a hall up here. It was in the same building that burned here, you know, it was the store and the bank and so on. And then upstairs was this big hall and they used to have quite a few dances up there, it was public, but of course you paid a few cents, you know, to come in there to dance. And they generally hire an orchestra, but it wasn't quite the same as it was out in the country. Out in the country they made their own. It'd be always some guy in the neighborhood, you know, could fiddle a little bit and there'd always be somebody in the neighborhood that could cord a little on the organ and that was it. Boy, they had lots of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go out to some of those from here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was the fire that burned the store down?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Gosh, I don't remember what year that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, just roughly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, the 30s, 40s?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, let's see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was about, I don't know, 12, 13 years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, that recent?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Mm-hmm. Of course, they didn't have, the last years there, when they had their store here, it wasn't run and it wasn't anywhere near like what we'd just been talking about. But, you know, it is run differently. The fact of the matter is they didn't do any wholesale business here. Oh, I think all of that stopped about in the 30s, you know, about the time of the Depression. Well, they quit that wholesale business and they quit most of the other business too and it was just strictly a retail store. But from the time it was started up until about the 30s, well then it was really a booming thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it run as a part of the lumber company?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, well, it was, you might say, a subsidiary. It was owned by the lumber company. But then, of course, it was, the business title was the Potlach-Mercantile Company, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you yourself would feel that the objections, which I've heard from, you know, once or twice, that the store prices were higher because it was the only store that you could go to and a monopoly, that those aren't really, it really wasn't anything.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, I wouldn't say so. No, because, you know, I think a person ought to be fair.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, when you try to analyze human nature, there are some people that are unhappy no matter what's done for them or anything else. And, you know, there were so many people that benefited financially and every other way from this operation here that I'd say that it more than offset anything that might have been a little bit objectionable. But, after all, no matter where you are, you've got to work. No matter what your job is, you've got to perform on that job or you just can't stay.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, you take, there were so many people that come in here that had such a low,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: well, their standard of living was so low that when they come in here, no matter what they got, it was a big boost, a big improvement, you know. And, personally, I can't look at it any other way, but it was a terribly big, just a big asset to the whole country. Now, you take, without this plant here, and without the people of this town, for a good many years, Moscow wouldn't have been anything outside of the fact that they've got the university. And, the same with Palouse and the whole area around here. You take the people come here with absolutely nothing and they bought land and they built their homes and they cleared their land, but they kept on working down here and they had a steady income. They had a steady payroll every month of the year, 12 months in a year for as many years as they stayed. And, you take that income, it's bound to leach out into the whole area. It doesn't make any difference if it's a gasoline station or if it's a bank or store or anything else. Everybody will draw some benefits from it because you take, you might say, you know, when you get into million dollar payrolls or higher, you know, the whole area is going to benefit from that. You take like the city of Lewiston, Lewiston is one of the oldest trading centers in the whole Northwest. You know, when they discovered gold up in the Dakotas, they outfitted here at Lewiston and they freighted by pack mule team from Lewiston clear back to the Dakotas. But, from that day on, now Lewiston, you see, is over 100 years old. But, up until the time that Potlatch built their sawmill down there, Lewiston never grew one particle. It is the same stagnant town that it was 75 years before that. Never grew at all. But, when they put their mill in there, now Lewiston takes in about four times the area that it had at that time. So, you can see how the city has expanded and it's all from that payroll and, of course, that payroll there brings more money into their banks. They build new banks, they build new stores, they build new this and that and everything else. But, it all comes from, that's the heart of the whole thing right out there and that's the way it was here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Many of the people that worked in the mill had their own little stump farms out here.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, that was one of the nice things about it. Now, you see, Potlatch owned practically all the surrounding country. Of course, there was a lot of farmers that had homesteaded, you know, and lived here before that Potlatch didn't own.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, most of them were out here in the prairie and so, as Potlatch logged this land off, after they took the logs off, then they sold this land for $10 an acre. They sold land for $400 for 40 acres. And, these people that worked down here at the mill, they could buy 40 acres or 80 acres or 120 or 160, whatever amount that they wanted to buy. And, all it took was one tenth down payment on that. Well, now you see, if they were going to buy 40 acres, all they had to pay down was $40. And, they could buy 40 acres, see. Well, then they could buy lumber and they could build a pretty decent house for about $300 or maybe less. Depends on the size of the house they wanted to build. And, they got that under the same terms. And, they kept on working and every payday they'd pay in a little bit on that until they'd own it, see. Well, that's all it, that's a big benefit that was created by the company. You know, without the company, they wouldn't ever have gotten any of that.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, most of the people that decided to do that, people who had farmed previously coming out here, or worldwide, just saw mill guys and weren't getting into that. Well, I think there was a combination there. There were some that had previous farming experience. And, then others that, you know, there's always people no matter where you go or where you live, there's always people who are always wanting to get a home. And, of course, if they think they've got a piece of land, well, then, of course, they've got it. And, of course, as far as making a living on the land was concerned, why, they just couldn't possibly make it a living if they didn't have its job. That job is what was creating a living for them because that land wouldn't produce anything for several years, and maybe a good many years before that land produced anything to speak of.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There was an awful lot of work to do on that land to get it in production.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was stumpage?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah, just logged over, rough and brush and stumps. You can imagine what logged over land was. But, they would stack up this trash wood, and some of it they'd cut it up for their own wood, some of it they'd cut it up and sell it for wood, and some of it they'd just pile it up in big piles and burn it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any way to get it off the ground, see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, then, most of them, they'd take a harrow and scratch around the ground a little bit between the stumps and throw some seed down. They got enough so they got a little pasture there, you know, for pasture a cow and a team of horses or something. And then, every year they'd dig out a few more stumps or buy a little dynamite and shoot a few, and that's the way it goes. There isn't any of this overnight stuff, you know, in that if you go buy a piece of stump land, well, you've got a job ahead of you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We're talking about this land.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: This included pretty much the land out of Princeton and the land in Harvard and around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All that's part of this, isn't it?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I wouldn't say that Potlatch owned all of that land because there was a lot of privately owned land in between, but then they did own an awful lot of land. And even the land that was privately owned, why, when they put the mill in here, those people benefited because they sold their timber to Potlatch, you see. And they didn't even have to log it themselves. They just sold it and Potlatch went in and logged it. Or they could log it themselves and Potlatch would pay them for it, you know, as logs. Different ways that it could be handled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's another complaint, incidentally, that I have heard against the company with regard to selling and buying the stumpage when they first, you know, buying the timber when they first came in from private people. They may just be looking back on it, the value of the timber, but they feel that the company picked it up for little or nothing and that when they went and logged the timber, a lot of these farmers, let's say over around the Erie area, found they couldn't do anything, couldn't make any money doing that for them, you see. They logged the stumpage and hauled it out for them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, that's true, but then, you know, hindsight is so darn much better than foresight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, you can look back and say, well, now if I'd have done this or done that, we'd all be millionaires, wouldn't we?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But, you know, oh, I get kind of teed off and sometimes amused at some of our politicians. Now, it's just a few years back, oh, within the last ten years, I'd say, or so, this Nez Perce tribe of Indians down here, around Lapwai and down in that part of the country, they got a hold of some of the politicians and told them that by getting the stumpage, they could do anything. And it's pretty golly that they had to get another settlement for the government because the government took over all this, so much of this land and they just paid them so much for it. And now then, that land around Lewiston and on there, people are paying $1 ,000, $2 ,000 an acre for it. So they ought to be reimbursed for what the government cheated them, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, isn't that a stupid thing?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But then the politicians say, oh, yes, you bet, they shake their heads off. Here, about, it's been about ten years ago, they made a settlement, one settlement with them there. I think they paid them an additional $17 million or something like that to that Lapwai tribe. And that was, for gold, it was supposed to have been taken out of the creeks down there, 100 or 150 years ago, you see. But now, with the price of gold up, we're supposed to pay the Indians a difference for what it was worth then and what it's worth now. And the same for the value of their land. Well, good heavens, at that time, you could buy all the cockeyed land that you wanted to surround for ten cents an acre.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take on them hills down there in that part of the country, why, they wouldn't even give ten cents an acre for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, of course, where I think they have just grievances, and a lot of those initial treaty obligations that the government agreed to when they signed them, they never did come through on them. Or, if they did, money went all into the pocket of the white Indian agent. That really is true.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They had promises of schools, medical facilities, all kinds of things in some of those first treaties just to get them signed. It looked good, but that was the end of it. Well, of course, I think we've got to recognize that, too, that they just figured that they were dealing with savages that didn't know anything and never would know anything. But, as far as our government was concerned, I think our government was honest in itself, but the representatives of the government were the ones that were just a lot of crooks and deadbeats, you know, that any way that they could promote a swindle. And, of course, that's carrying on right up until today. They're doing the same thing every day back in Washington and in every state in the Union, as far as that's concerned, wherever they get a chance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, Axel Anderson told me how he decided to come to work for Potlatch, was he was looking at the board in Spokane to see what was available for work. This was in 1970. And he said he'd already been on a few jobs, a lot of jobs, in fact, where he had worked with the wages, been pretty good, and just didn't like it. You know, conditions were bad, one reason or another, he just didn't like it. So he said this time he decided he'd find a place that offered the least wages on the board and go there and try that and see if it was any better. And he said that was Potlatch at the time, at least compared to these others. But I guess he decided that what they give you in the salary alone didn't maybe mean all that much. It might be other things about the people you were working for that would make it a place you could stay and work for.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course, you take, as far as Axel Anderson is concerned, he worked in the woods all his life. And if he was ever in a lumber camp, and you'd see how some of those people eat, and see the food that is prepared for them, you could see why they wanted to make that their way of life. Because, boy, they really did eat. Now you take back in the days before refrigeration, why it was pretty hard to keep meat especially. But they were pretty ingenious in some ways. Now I've been in some of the Potlatch logging camps and they didn't have any refrigeration. But their meat house was constructed of canvas. It had a wood floor and low wood side walls in it, but then it was just a big tent deal. But they kept a stream of water running over this on the outside all the time, day and night. That took care of all the evaporation. It was a cooler in itself. They had a little office, you see. And they could keep meat fresh in there for several days. And of course their cured meats like hams and bacons and stuff, that would keep for quite a while. And their eggs and all that stuff they kept in there. And of course their supply was replenished just every few days. But they didn't have the contamination and rotten conditions like they had in some camps. They were in pretty good shape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time period is this you're talking about?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: About 1920 and around in there.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of course at that time, all the Potlatch camps, they furnished all the beds and blankets and all that stuff.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: About three or four years before that, that was when the so-called Wobblies were credited with a lot of stuff that I don't know, I guess maybe the creditors do. But they did make conditions a lot better in the camps. You take prior to World War I, when the man went to work in the logging camp, he carried his own bedroll on his back. And of course they had bunks, but then the bunks were all empty. You'd go down to the barn and get an armful of straw or two armsful of whatever you wanted and put in there and put your bed on top of it. That was your bed.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, you could come from home and your wife had your bedding all washed up and your clothes all washed up and clean. You were just as clean as a button from one end to the other. And you carried your bed into the camp, spread it out, and you went to work. And when you come home, then the next weekend, you'd be so lousy that your bed would walk off by itself. But that wasn't the lice that you brought in, it was the lice this other dude brought in, the guy that never did wash or clean up. And that's one thing too that the Wobblies should be credited with. And that was washrooms. After they got them, they insisted that some of these guys had never had a bath in their life. By golly, they had to bathe and they had to clean their clothes and stuff. And they got rid of these lice. But years ago, it used to be terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what the men did to fight that kind of thing? Fight the lice?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, you had to do it. You were living in the camp. But then one thing that they'd done was, well, of course, some of them, they would take their clothes and they'd build a fire outside and they'd have a great big kettle out there or boiler or barrel or something, you know. And they'd fill that with water on this fire and they'd boil their clothes. They didn't boil them. Well, you know what that'll do to wool, is just ruin their wool clothes. Of course, they all wore wool. And then, of course, there was others, too. You could hear lots of different ways. Others, you know, they'd take off their undershirt and they'd turn their shirt inside out and they'd go down to the barn and they'd lay it on the horse's back. Well, all the lice would leave his shirt and he'd get on the horse. All them gimmicks, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? Have you ever heard about carrying mercury in your pocket?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's over, I've heard that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For lice?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. Keep them off you. Well, maybe it would, but I don't know how it would. Mercury's a darn hard to hold. And if you've got, well, all the mercury that I've seen, they generally had mercury in a little crockery, kind of a glazed crockery jug, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But how that, how that, of course there could be some radiation from it, I don't know. But, you know, if you pour a little mercury out in your hand and you go to pick up, you've got a ball there, but you go to pick it up, then you've got a hundred balls and you go to pick them up, you've got a thousand balls. That's the way mercury does, you know, and it's pretty hard to handle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you heard about rubbing with salves?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I've heard that too, that some guys use salves. Well, I suppose there were some that, you know, carbolic salves, you know, that might help. But, there are lots of different gimmicks.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But you were trying to say that the poo was really quite good in the camps during that, during that bus. Oh, yeah. Yeah, you take,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: well, you know, they, they charge a man so much a day for his board, but then you'd probably pay four times that much if you were to get the same food in the restaurant someplace.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, oh, I've seen men that, oh, they'd settle for a bowl of corn flakes and maybe an egg and a piece of toast and that would be enough for their breakfast. And then the person, maybe the next guy to them, he'd want a big steak and four or five eggs and some cereal and, gosh, you name it, and that'd be his breakfast. Well, then they'd come in and most of them would carry a lunch, but then when they, every man made up his own lunch. Well, in the cookhouse they had, oh, big drawers about four feet wide and ten, twelve inches deep. And they'd pull these out and this drawer would be full of one kind of cookies and that drawer would be full of another kind of cookies and that other drawer is full of donuts. And this drawer over here may be full of cake and, and then, of course, on top there'd be great big tables just stacked full of big plates of cheese and all kinds, every kind of lunch meat you can think of. And then you know, you know what boiled ham is, the most expensive kind of lunch meat that you can buy. Well, some fellas would take, wouldn't take a few slices of that for their lunch. They'd take a whole can, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And, you know, that's just the way some, and then of course some of them, you know, too, I suppose, maybe would stash enough of that away to when they'd go home on weekends, they'd take it home with them. I don't know, but then I don't see how they could possibly eat that much.
